categoryReason: The commit message explicitly states the goal is to 'Replace Boolean wrapper object by boolean primitive type when an object is not necessary', indicating a performance and type clarity improvement.
dslRule:
<!id: wrapper.to.primitive.Boolean.declaration>
<!description: Replace Boolean wrapper local variable declaration with boolean primitive type if initializer is non-null and subsequent usages are compatible.>
<!severity: info>
java.lang.Boolean $var = $init;
:: isAssignedToLocalVariable() && isNonNull($init)
=> boolean $var = $init;
;;

<!id: wrapper.to.primitive.Byte.declaration>
<!description: Replace Byte wrapper local variable declaration with byte primitive type if initializer is non-null and subsequent usages are compatible.>
<!severity: info>
java.lang.Byte $var = $init;
:: isAssignedToLocalVariable() && isNonNull($init)
=> byte $var = $init;
;;

<!id: wrapper.to.primitive.Short.declaration>
<!description: Replace Short wrapper local variable declaration with short primitive type if initializer is non-null and subsequent usages are compatible.>
<!severity: info>
java.lang.Short $var = $init;
:: isAssignedToLocalVariable() && isNonNull($init)
=> short $var = $init;
;;

<!id: wrapper.to.primitive.Character.declaration>
<!description: Replace Character wrapper local variable declaration with char primitive type if initializer is non-null and subsequent usages are compatible.>
<!severity: info>
java.lang.Character $var = $init;
:: isAssignedToLocalVariable() && isNonNull($init)
=> char $var = $init;
;;

<!id: wrapper.to.primitive.Integer.declaration>
<!description: Replace Integer wrapper local variable declaration with int primitive type if initializer is non-null and subsequent usages are compatible.>
<!severity: info>
java.lang.Integer $var = $init;
:: isAssignedToLocalVariable() && isNonNull($init)
=> int $var = $init;
;;

<!id: wrapper.to.primitive.Long.declaration>
<!description: Replace Long wrapper local variable declaration with long primitive type if initializer is non-null and subsequent usages are compatible.>
<!severity: info>
java.lang.Long $var = $init;
:: isAssignedToLocalVariable() && isNonNull($init)
=> long $var = $init;
;;

<!id: wrapper.to.primitive.Float.declaration>
<!description: Replace Float wrapper local variable declaration with float primitive type if initializer is non-null and subsequent usages are compatible.>
<!severity: info>
java.lang.Float $var = $init;
:: isAssignedToLocalVariable() && isNonNull($init)
=> float $var = $init;
;;

<!id: wrapper.to.primitive.Double.declaration>
<!description: Replace Double wrapper local variable declaration with double primitive type if initializer is non-null and subsequent usages are compatible.>
<!severity: info>
java.lang.Double $var = $init;
:: isAssignedToLocalVariable() && isNonNull($init)
=> double $var = $init;
;;
summary: This commit primarily refactors the internal implementation of JDT's 'Primitive rather than wrapper' cleanup by consolidating multiple individual cleanups (for Double, Long, Short, Integer) into a single, generic `PrimitiveRatherThanWrapperCleanUp`. While the commit itself is an architectural change to the cleanup infrastructure, the underlying transformation it performs—replacing wrapper object local variable declarations with their primitive types—is a reusable code pattern. The proposed DSL rules capture this core transformation for all primitive wrapper types (Boolean, Byte, Short, Character, Integer, Long, Float, Double) in local variable declarations. Each rule is guarded by `isAssignedToLocalVariable()` and `isNonNull($init)` to ensure basic semantic safety (e.g., preventing NullPointerExceptions). The full 'should not make more autoboxing/unboxing' condition mentioned in the commit message, which requires advanced type-flow analysis of all variable usages, is beyond the current DSL's capabilities. However, these rules accurately reflect the core type change and handle explicit non-null initializers, making them a GREEN transformation for the directly verifiable aspects.

commitMessage:
Bug 573220 - NPE in JavaMethodCompletionProposal

- fix canAutomaticallyAppendSemicolon() method to handle the case
  where the invocation context does not have a compilation unit set

Change-Id: I0d1cddfc12e9f560afb5527a0322ecd8e52830e1
Reviewed-on: https://git.eclipse.org/r/c/jdt/eclipse.jdt.ui/+/179960
Tested-by: JDT Bot <jdt-bot@eclipse.org>
Reviewed-by: Andrey Loskutov <loskutov@gmx.de>
categoryReason: The change is a bug fix within an internal Eclipse JDT UI component, addressing a NullPointerException.
languageChangeNeeded: The DSL would need to support matching an expression (e.g., '$cu.getElementAt($offset)') and replacing it with a surrounding conditional statement (e.g., 'if ($cu != null) { $cu.getElementAt($offset) ... }'). This involves inserting control flow and modifying the structure around matched code, which is not currently supported at the expression rewrite level. It would require advanced structural AST manipulation capabilities, potentially with semantic nullability analysis to detect the missing check.
summary: This commit fixes a NullPointerException in `JavaMethodCompletionProposal.canAutomaticallyAppendSemicolon()` by adding a null check for the `ICompilationUnit` obtained from the invocation context. The change involves wrapping several lines of existing code within an `if (cu != null)` block. This is a structural code insertion that cannot be expressed with the current TriggerPattern DSL, which focuses on pattern matching and replacement of expressions or simple statement continuations, not arbitrary control flow insertion around matched code. Hence, it is classified as RED.

categoryReason: The change improves code correctness by consolidating null checks to prevent potential issues and SpotBugs warnings.
languageChangeNeeded: The DSL would need support for more advanced control flow analysis and statement relocation/restructuring. The current DSL primarily supports expression/statement replacements at the matched node's location, not moving entire blocks of code based on complex conditional logic across a method.
summary: The commit consolidates null checks for a 'binding' variable in `LocalCorrectionsSubProcessor.java`. Instead of having multiple `if (binding == null) return;` statements within a conditional block, the check is moved to a single point after the block. This is a structural refactoring to improve code clarity and prevent SpotBugs warnings. The TriggerPattern DSL, in its current form, does not support relocating statements or complex control flow restructuring based on analysis of multiple conditional branches. It's a RED transformation, requiring significant DSL enhancements.

commitMessage:
Bug 573289 - [cleanup] String.strip().isEmpty() => String.isBlank()

String.isBlank() rather than String.strip().isEmpty()

String.isBlank() is shorter than checking the stripped string or
checking the length of the stripped string and it avoids the creation of
an intermediate trimmed string.

To apply the cleanup also to String.trim().isEmpty() (which is probably
more common than String.strip().isEmpty(), since strip() exists only
since Java 11), trim() must first be replaced by strip() which may
change the runtime behavior.

Given:
if (input.strip().isEmpty()) {
    System.err.println("Input must not be blank");
}
boolean hasComment = comment.strip().length() > 0;

When:
Clean up the code enabling "String.isBlank() rather than
String.strip().isEmpty()" and using Java 11 or higher

Then:
if (input.isBlank()) {
    System.err.println("Input must not be blank");
}
boolean hasComment = !comment.isBlank();

Also use Java 12 Runtime jar for the Java 11 tests instead of Java 10
Runtime jar

Change-Id: I072b6e18fa951ab9901e3363b55be2c18654888f
Signed-off-by: Holger Voormann <eclipse@voormann.de>
Reviewed-on: https://git.eclipse.org/r/c/jdt/eclipse.jdt.ui/+/180067
Tested-by: JDT Bot <jdt-bot@eclipse.org>
Reviewed-by: Fabrice Tiercelin <fabrice.tiercelin@yahoo.fr>
categoryReason: Modernization of String API usage.
summary: This commit introduces a new JDT cleanup that replaces various patterns for checking if a string is blank (using `strip().isEmpty()`, `strip().length() == 0`, `equals("")` with `strip()`) with the more concise `String.isBlank()`. This modernization improves readability and avoids intermediate string allocations. The transformation is applicable for Java 11 and higher. While some patterns (e.g., `strip().isEmpty()`) are already partially covered, this proposal extends `string-isblank.sandbox-hint` to comprehensively cover `stripLeading()`, `stripTrailing()`, and the full range of `length()` comparisons for all `strip()` methods. All identified patterns are direct 1:1 expression replacements, fully supported by the TriggerPattern DSL.

commitMessage:

Therefore, this transformation is classified as RED, requiring significant extensions to the DSL's core capabilities. This functionality is typically handled by existing Java-based cleanups for unused local variables.

commitMessage:
Bug 572261 - [cleanup] Provide StringBuffer to StringBuilder cleanup

- create a cleanup to replace usage of StringBuffer with
  StringBuilder.  The two are equivalent, but StringBuffer is
  thread-safe and synchronized which makes it slower than
  StringBuilder which was introduced in Java 1.5.

  There are two sub-options of the clean-up.  By default,
  the clean-up is for local variables only which means it will
  not modify fields or method declarations/calls so changing
  a single class will not affect other classes that reference it.

  The other sub-option is to change all references of StringBuffer
  to StringBuilder including method declarations, fields, Javadoc,
  etc...  In the second case, callers and users of fields, methods
  are not changed unless they are included as part of the clean-up
  (e.g. performing clean-up on a package or project(s)).

Change-Id: Ib6fde36986c09aac2d1a6e1a02c05158198063ce
Reviewed-on: https://git.eclipse.org/r/c/jdt/eclipse.jdt.ui/+/181118
Tested-by: JDT Bot <jdt-bot@eclipse.org>
Tested-by: Jeff Johnston <jjohnstn@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeff Johnston <jjohnstn@redhat.com>
categoryReason: The transformation from StringBuffer to StringBuilder is a standard performance optimization in Java.
summary: This commit introduces a new Java-based cleanup for replacing `StringBuffer` with `StringBuilder`. The core transformation pattern, focusing on local variables for semantic safety, is already perfectly covered by existing GREEN TriggerPattern DSL rules in `stringbuffer-to-stringbuilder.sandbox-hint`. The commit itself adds the Java cleanup implementation, but the underlying transformation logic is fully expressible and already exists in the DSL.

commitMessage:
Bug 573980: [17][quick fix] Provide quick fix to declare sealed
interface as super interface of permitted types

Change-Id: I6cc326b83cb489f8db12a806abaca98c4de2273f
Reviewed-on: https://git.eclipse.org/r/c/jdt/eclipse.jdt.ui/+/182108
Tested-by: JDT Bot <jdt-bot@eclipse.org>
Reviewed-by: Noopur Gupta <noopur_gupta@in.ibm.com>
categoryReason: This commit introduces a new quick fix functionality to support Java 17 sealed interfaces, rather than fixing an existing quick fix. While the category 'Quick Fix Bug Fix' exists, this is a new feature.
languageChangeNeeded: The DSL needs to support arbitrary AST node insertion (e.g., creating a new Type node and inserting it into a list property like `TypeDeclaration.SUPER_INTERFACE_TYPES_PROPERTY`) and more complex AST traversal/conditional logic during the rewrite phase. Current DSL is focused on pattern-matching and replacement of existing expressions, statements, or annotations.
summary: This commit introduces a new quick fix to declare a sealed interface as a super-interface of a permitted type, supporting Java 17 sealed classes. The implementation involves complex AST traversal, binding resolution, and the dynamic insertion of new AST nodes (TypeReference) into a list property (`TypeDeclaration.SUPER_INTERFACE_TYPES_PROPERTY`). This level of arbitrary AST manipulation and control flow logic is beyond the current capabilities of the TriggerPattern DSL, which focuses on pattern matching and replacement of existing expressions, statements, or annotations. Therefore, it is classified as RED.

commitMessage:
Bug 573980: [17] Do not provide quick fix if permitted type is not
present in a compilation unit (e.g. is binary / non-editable)


Change-Id: Ib649988e1149c7f36130357aa929ecd235f5ccb9
Reviewed-on: https://git.eclipse.org/r/c/jdt/eclipse.jdt.ui/+/182390
Tested-by: JDT Bot <jdt-bot@eclipse.org>
Reviewed-by: Noopur Gupta <noopur_gupta@in.ibm.com>
categoryReason: The change adds a null check to prevent a potential NullPointerException, falling under defensive programming and null-safety.
languageChangeNeeded: The DSL needs support for inserting arbitrary statement blocks, including if-statements and return statements, as replacements. Currently, replacements are limited to expressions or simple line continuations.
summary: This commit fixes a bug (Bug 573980) by adding a null check for an `ICompilationUnit` variable and an early return, specifically to address issues with non-editable permitted types in Java 17. While the pattern of adding defensive null checks is highly reusable, the TriggerPattern DSL currently does not support inserting new control flow structures (like an `if` statement with a `return` block) as part of a transformation. This would require significant extensions to the DSL's capabilities for statement-level rewrites, classifying it as RED.

commitMessage:
Bug 574376 - [dogfooding][cleanup] Use new StringBuffer -> StringBuilder
cleanup

Runs the new StringBuffer-> StringBuilder

This help to determine that it works correctly for clients we should run
it also on our own code base. This help to ensure that the cleanup works
fine and helps us to cleanup our own code base.

From the commit message of the cleanup:

- create a cleanup to replace usage of StringBuffer with
  StringBuilder.  The two are equivalent, but StringBuffer is
  thread-safe and synchronized which makes it slower than
  StringBuilder which was introduced in Java 1.5.


This uses the local variable change only.

Change-Id: Iec3f968dd879c63b3e108c31a5a6e8178b8617bf
Signed-off-by: Lars Vogel <Lars.Vogel@vogella.com>
Reviewed-on: https://git.eclipse.org/r/c/jdt/eclipse.jdt.ui/+/182327
Tested-by: JDT Bot <jdt-bot@eclipse.org>
Reviewed-by: Jeff Johnston <jjohnstn@redhat.com>
categoryReason: The change improves performance by replacing a synchronized class with an unsynchronized one.
summary: This commit systematically replaces `java.lang.StringBuffer` with `java.lang.StringBuilder` for local variable declarations and initializations. `StringBuilder` is a more performant, unsynchronized alternative suitable when thread-safety is not a concern. The transformation explicitly targets local variables, ensuring semantic safety by using the `isAssignedToLocalVariable()` guard, as indicated in the commit message. This pattern is a direct 1:1 replacement of constructor calls for different argument counts and is fully covered by existing DSL rules in `stringbuffer-to-stringbuilder.sandbox-hint`, making it a GREEN and duplicate transformation. It improves performance and aligns with modern Java best practices.

commitMessage:
Bug 574526 - [17][quick fix] Provide quick fix to add a type to
permitted types of a sealed type

Change-Id: I574ba1be0ad691f5fd3c0a032fb0cff486c05099
Signed-off-by: Kalyan Prasad Tatavarthi <kalyan_prasad@in.ibm.com>
Reviewed-on: https://git.eclipse.org/r/c/jdt/eclipse.jdt.ui/+/182694
Tested-by: JDT Bot <jdt-bot@eclipse.org>
categoryReason: This commit introduces new quick-fix logic for a Java 17 feature (sealed types). It's distinct from general refactoring, performance, or deprecation fix categories.
languageChangeNeeded: The DSL needs capabilities for complex AST node creation and insertion into NodeList properties (e.g., TypeDeclaration.PERMITS_TYPES_PROPERTY). This would require extending the replacement side of the DSL to allow more imperative-like AST builder operations.
summary: This commit introduces a new quick-fix to add a type to the `permits` clause of a sealed type (Java 17+). The core change involves programmatically creating and inserting an AST `Type` node into the `PERMITS_TYPES_PROPERTY` list of a `TypeDeclaration`. This type of structural AST manipulation, including node creation and list modification, is currently beyond the capabilities of the TriggerPattern DSL, which focuses on expression-level and annotation replacements. The logic is part of Eclipse's internal quick-fix infrastructure. Therefore, the transformation is classified as RED. Minor internal string literal changes are not considered as generalizable patterns.
